# Mathmos Lava Lamp Heritage

![mathmos lava lamp inventor edward craven walker poole uk](https://network.mathmos.com/app/uploads/sites/10/fly-images/2369/3_mathmos_lava_lamp_inventor_edward_craven_walker_poole_uk-784x570-c.jpg)

## Mathmos Lava Lamps in the 1960s & 70s

The Mathmos story begins with the invention of the Astro lava lamp in 1963 by our founder Edward Craven Walker. Craven Walker was a great British inventor, entrepreneur and eccentric. Aside from inventing the lava lamp and various other patented inventions, he also made underwater naturist films, owned helicopters and fire engines, was a Second World War pilot, entertained the cast of the 60s musical Hair and owned a naturist camp in Dorset.

[Find out more](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps-in-the-1960s-70s/)

![mathmos lava lamp heritage 90s](https://network.mathmos.com/app/uploads/sites/10/fly-images/2250/01_mathmos_lava_lamps_heritage_90s_rebrand-784x570-c.jpg)

## Mathmos Lava Lamps in the 1990s

The Craven Walkers wanting to retire, teamed up with young entrepreneurs Cressida Granger and David Mulley in 1989, who launched the lava lamp to a new generation. Part of the relaunch included changing the company name from Crestworth to Mathmos in 1992. The word Mathmos is taken from the cult 1960s film Barbarella. Millions of Mathmos lava lamps were manufactured in Dorset in this period and sold throughout the world, gaining a host of business and marketing awards, including two Queens Awards for Export.

[Find out more](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps-in-the-1990s/)

![Mathmos Lava Lamps - 00s to Now](https://network.mathmos.com/app/uploads/sites/10/fly-images/4425/Mathmos_iO_giant_lava_lamp_silver_2ndhome_orangewall_violet_red-784x570-c.jpg)

## Mathmos Lava Lamps 00s to now

Mathmos have designed and developed many award-winning lamps over the years. Innovations in lava lamps include our range of candle powered lava lamps, our new generation child safe lava lamp Neo and Neo wall, the only wall-mounted lava lamp, together with our range of giant lava lamps. Our factory is still based in Poole, Dorset where our lamps have been in continuous production since their invention in 1963. We are proud of our long British manufacturing history, our high quality and our heritage of invention and design.

[Find out more](https://mathmos.com/00s-to-now/)

## Watch Mathmos Timeline

Mathmos was founded in Britain in 1963 by Edward Craven Walker, inventor of the original lava lamp.

Mathmos lava lamps became symbols of the creative culture of the 1960s and 70s and returned to huge popularity in the 1990s.

Today Mathmos lava lamps enjoy an iconic status and have an enduring place in popular culture.

> I think it will always be popular. It’s like the cycle of life. It grows, breaks up, falls down and then starts all over again

Edward Craven Walker 1918 -2000

HelpHelp

[Delivery & Returns](https://mathmos.com/delivery-and-returns/)

- Where can I buy replacement parts for my Mathmos lava lamp?







We offer a range of replacement parts for many current and recent models, including [bottles](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bottles/), [bulbs](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bulbs/), [bases, caps, and other small parts](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/bases-caps-small-parts/).



Mathmos lava lamps are designed to last, with replacement parts available for many models. If you cannot find the part you need, please contact our [customer service](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/) team.

- Can I exchange a replacement bottle that does not fit my lava lamp?







If your replacement bottle does not fit your lamp, please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/) with your order number and details of your lamp. Subject to our [Returns Policy](https://mathmos.com/delivery-and-returns/), we can advise on exchange options.

- Why is there a metal spring or coil in my lava lamp bottle?







The metal spring at the bottom of your lava lamp bottle is an essential part of the lamp and helps the lava flow correctly. If the spring becomes dislodged, run the lamp for up to six hours to allow it to settle back into place. This may need to be repeated if the spring does not settle on the first attempt.

- What makes a Mathmos lava lamp different from other lava lamps?







Mathmos has been manufacturing high quality lava lamps in Britain since 1963. Our lamps feature a unique lava formulation developed to deliver superior flow, longer life, clearer liquid and more vivid colours. We also offer a comprehensive range of [spare parts](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/) to help keep your lamp performing at its best for years to come. Every Mathmos lava lamp is designed to meet the highest safety standards and undergoes regular independent testing to ensure exceptional quality.

- Why is my lava lamp forming stalagmite shapes?







Stalagmite shapes are a normal part of the lava lamp warming up process. As the lamp heats up, these shapes will gradually melt and transform into the flowing lava effect. Once the lamp has reached its optimum operating temperature, the lava should begin to flow normally.

- Why does my lava lamp look a different colour from the photos?







Mathmos lava lamp bottles are handcrafted, so slight variations in colour can occur. More commonly, the appearance of a lava lamp can vary depending on room lighting and screen display settings. Our product photos are taken to represent the colours as accurately as possible, but colours may appear different under different lighting conditions. For more information on how lighting can affect the appearance of your lava lamp, please see our blog article [The Appearance of Colour](https://mathmos.com/the-appearance-of-colour/).

- What is Mathmos doing about the halogen bulb phase-out?







Mathmos continues to use compliant halogen bulbs to heat and light its lava lamps, despite the phase-out of some halogen bulbs for general lighting applications. We are also exploring alternative technologies for future lava lamp designs should regulations change further. Our commitment is to keep Mathmos lava lamps in use for as long as possible by continuing to offer [spare parts](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/), [replacement bulbs](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bulbs/) and ongoing support wherever possible.

- How long does a Mathmos lava lamp bottle or bulb last?







Mathmos lava lamp bottles typically provide around 2,000 hours of operation, although many customers enjoy their bottles for much longer. The lifespan of a bulb can vary depending on usage and handling. Bulbs purchased directly from Mathmos are covered by a one year guarantee. Replacement [bottles](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bottles/) and [bulbs](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bulbs/) are available on our website for many current and recent models.

- How long should I leave my lava lamp on?







Most Mathmos lava lamps can be used for up to six hours at a time. Exceptions include the Mathmos [Candle lava lamps](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/candle-powered/), which should be used for no more than four hours, and the Mathmos [Saturn](https://mathmos.com/product/saturn-giant-lava-lamp/) and [Jumbo Jet](https://mathmos.com/product/jumbo-jet-giant-lava-lamp/) Giant lava lamps, which can be used for up to twelve hours. Always follow the operating instructions supplied with your lava lamp.

- Where can I find the Mathmos logo on my lava lamp?







The Mathmos logo can be found in several locations depending on the model, including the base, cap, cord label and, on most models, etched onto the bottle. Some bottles also feature the Mathmos name or logo moulded into the glass on the underside.

- Why does my lava lamp have specks or bubbles in the glass?







Mathmos lava lamp bottles are made in one of the highest quality automated glass factories in Great Britain. Small bubbles and specks in the glass are a normal part of the manufacturing process and are not a fault. They do not affect the performance, safety or lifespan of your lava lamp.

- Why is the wax in my lava lamp stuck at the top?







A small amount of wax at the top of your lava lamp bottle can occur as the lamp cools between uses and is not usually a cause for concern. If a large amount of wax remains stuck at the top during operation, the lamp may have overheated. Turn the lamp off and allow it to cool completely before using it again.

- Can I buy Mathmos lamps and spare parts in the USA or Canada?







We currently don't upply the USA or Canada. However, a small range of our iconic Mathmos lamps may become available in the future. To stay in the loop, sign up to our mailing list [here](https://mathmos.com/Mathmos-usa/).



_NB. Schylling Inc. owns the "LAVA" trademark in the USA and Canada. Mathmos Ltd is totally independent and not connected to Schylling Inc in any way._

- Why is the liquid in my lava lamp cloudy?







A cloudy lava lamp bottle is usually caused by the bottle being shaken or moved while hot, or by the bottle reaching the end of its lifespan. If your lava lamp is cloudy, run it through a few normal operating cycles to see if the liquid clears. If the cloudiness does not improve, a [replacement bottle](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bottles/) may be required and can be purchased from our website.

- Why has my lava lamp bottle faded?







The colour of a lava lamp bottle can gradually fade over time due to age, heat and exposure to light. To help preserve the colour of your lava lamp, keep it away from direct sunlight, strong natural light and other heat sources. If your lava lamp bottle is still under warranty and you have followed the care instructions, please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/). Otherwise, a [replacement bottle](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bottles/) can be purchased from our website.

- Can I order a lava lamp colour that is not shown on your website?







Mathmos has offered a wide variety of lava lamp colours throughout its history. While it is not possible for us to keep every colour in stock, we continue to offer a carefully selected range of lava lamp colours. We also occasionally release new colours and [Special Fills](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamp-colours-mathmos-special-fills-explained/), so we recommend signing up to our [mailing list](https://mathmos.us3.list-manage.com/subscribe/post?u=cb9394b9c4b6ddd0a6a43cd62&id=4180d60836&v_id=452&f_id=0087c2e1f0) and following us on [social media](https://www.instagram.com/mathmos_originals/) for the latest updates.

- Does Mathmos offer custom lava lamp bottle colours?







Mathmos does not offer custom lava lamp bottle colours for most models. However, custom bottle colours may be available for [Saturn](https://mathmos.com/product/saturn-giant-lava-lamp/) and [Jumbo Jet](https://mathmos.com/product/jumbo-jet-giant-lava-lamp/) Giant lava lamps. Please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/) to discuss custom colour options for these models.

- Can I buy replacement liquid for my lava lamp?







Mathmos lava lamp bottles are sealed units and should not be opened or refilled. Attempting to add replacement liquid will void your warranty and may cause overfilling, increasing pressure on the glass bottle. For safety reasons, we do not sell lava lamp liquid separately. Instead, you can purchase a [replacement lava lamp bottle](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bottles/) with professionally filled contents from the Mathmos website.

- Why can I see air bubbles in the lava?







Small air bubbles in the lava are normal, especially when you first receive your lava lamp. These bubbles will generally reduce as the lava lamp is used over time and do not affect performance.

- Why is the wax in my lava lamp stuck to the glass?







If the wax in your lava lamp is stuck to the glass, try using the lamp a few more times. In most cases, the wax will gradually rejoin the rest of the contents. If the wax remains stuck and you have followed the operating instructions, please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/). If your lava lamp is no longer under warranty, a [replacement bottle](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bottles/) may be required.

- Why can I see particles in my new lava lamp bottle?







There should be nothing in your lava lamp bottle besides the lava, liquid and metal coil. Occasionally, you may notice small particles in the lava as it flows. These are usually dye particles from the manufacturing process and are not considered a fault. In most cases, they will become less noticeable over time. If you see anything else in your lava lamp bottle, please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/).

- How should I recycle my lava lamp?







Before recycling your lava lamp, consider replacing parts such as the [bottle](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bottles/), [bulb](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bulbs/), [base and cap](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/bases-caps-small-parts/). We offer spares for most Mathmos lava lamp models from the 1960s to today. If you own a Giant lava lamp, please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/).



If recycling is necessary, please take the lamp to your nearest household recycling centre that accepts electrical waste.

- Has the formulation of Mathmos lava lamps changed?







The formula used in Mathmos lava lamps has evolved since the 1960s, improving resistance to clouding in heat and freezing in cold temperatures. However, the formula has remained unchanged in recent years.

- Why is my lava lamp not flowing?







If your lava lamp is not flowing:



  - Check that the bulb is working and is the correct wattage for your model.
  - Ensure the lamp is positioned away from drafts, air conditioning and windows.
  - Make sure the room temperature is at least 20°C.
  - Allow the lamp to run for up to 4 to 6 hours, as lava lamps can take longer to warm up when first used.
  - For older lava lamp bases, check that the correct bulb is fitted by referring to the bulb care and handling guidance for your model.
  - Ensure the metal coil is present at the bottom of the bottle, as this is essential for proper lava flow.

- How can I clean my lava lamp?







To clean your lava lamp, use a glass cleaner and a soft cloth on the lava lamp bottle. The base and cap are made from either aluminium or zinc alloy, depending on the model, so use cleaning products recommended for these materials. If you are unsure, use a soft cloth only. Avoid abrasive cleaning products, as they can permanently damage your lava lamp.

- What should I do if my lava lamp is not working?







Firstly, check our [FAQs](https://mathmos.com/faq/) to see if your lava lamp issue is covered. If you still need assistance, all Mathmos lava lamps include a one year warranty, extended to two years when purchased directly from our website. If your lava lamp is within the warranty period, please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/). For more information, please see our [Faulty Product](https://mathmos.com/delivery-and-returns/) returns advice.

- Can I buy a replacement screw cap for my lava lamp bottle?







No, replacement screw caps for Mathmos lava lamp bottles are not available separately. The bottle caps are glue sealed and pressure fitted during manufacture. If your bottle requires replacement, you can purchase a [replacement bottle](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bottles/) from the Mathmos website.

- What should I do if I accidentally break my lava lamp?







If your lava lamp bottle breaks, carefully collect any broken glass and dispose of it safely. We recommend wearing gloves to protect your hands. Use paper towels or a disposable cloth to absorb as much of the liquid and lava as possible, then clean the affected area using methods appropriate for the surface. For specific cleaning advice, refer to the manufacturer's recommendations for your surface material.

- How old is my lava lamp?







Due to changes in manufacturing over the years, it is not always possible to identify the exact age of a Mathmos lava lamp. If you would like to learn more about your lamp, specialist resources such as [flowoflava.com](https://www.imovatedesign.co.uk/start/) may be able to help.

- Can I buy a replacement lamp holder for my lava lamp?







Due to electrical safety regulations, we are unable to supply replacement lamp holders separately. However, replacement [base and cap](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/bases-caps-small-parts/) bundles are available for many models, and a wide range of other parts can be found in our [spares and extras](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/) section.

- How does a lava lamp work?







A lava lamp works by gently heating a special wax formulation at the base of the bottle. As the wax warms, it becomes less dense than the surrounding liquid and rises. When it cools, it becomes denser and sinks again, creating the smooth, continuous movement that defines a Mathmos lava lamp. Mathmos has refined its lava lamp formula since 1963 to improve clarity, colour stability and long-lasting flow.

- Can I move or shake my lava lamp?







No. Lava lamps should not be moved or shaken while operating or when the bottle is still warm. This can cause the liquid to become cloudy and may affect the flow of the lava. Always allow your lava lamp to cool completely before moving it.

- Why is my lava lamp not flowing after storage or shipping?







If your lava lamp is not flowing after storage or shipping, this is usually temporary. Following a long period of storage or transport, the wax may need several heating and cooling cycles to return to normal operation. Run your lava lamp for up to six hours at a time in a room between 20°C and 24°C. The flow should gradually improve as the wax and coil settle.

- Can I use any bulb in a lava lamp?







No. Lava lamps require a specific wattage and type of bulb to heat the wax correctly. Using the wrong bulb can prevent your lava lamp from flowing properly or may cause the bottle to overheat. LED bulbs are not suitable, as they do not produce the heat needed for proper flow. Please visit our [replacement bulbs](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bulbs/) section to find the correct bulb for your model.

- Are lava lamps safe around pets?







Yes, when used responsibly. Lava lamps should be kept out of reach of pets and placed on a stable surface. Like any electrical product, a lava lamp is best used under supervision.

- What should I do if my lava lamp is leaking?







If your lava lamp is leaking, switch it off and allow it to cool before handling. Wipe away any leaked liquid and check the bottle and base carefully. The liquid inside is not dangerous, but the lamp should not be used again until the cause of the leak has been identified.

- What are the benefits of a lava lamp?







Lava lamps offer a unique combination of ambient light, movement and colour. Their ever changing flow creates a captivating visual experience, making them popular for relaxation, creative spaces and interior design. Mathmos continues to make lava lamps in Britain today, drawing on decades of expertise.

- Do lava lamps help with ADHD?







Some people find the slow, repetitive movement of lava lamps soothing and visually engaging. Lava lamps are not a medical product, though they can contribute to a calm environment.

- What is the liquid inside a lava lamp?







The liquid inside a Mathmos lava lamp is a high quality, water based formula that provides clarity and smooth wax movement. The exact formulation is unique to Mathmos and has been refined over more than 60 years.

- Can I leave my lava lamp on all night?







No. We do not recommend leaving a lava lamp on all night. For best performance and longevity, operate your lava lamp for up to six hours at a time and allow it to cool completely before switching it on again.

- Do lava lamps use a lot of electricity?







Not really. Most Mathmos lava lamps use a low wattage bulb. The amount of electricity used depends on the model, bulb wattage and operating time.

- Will I have to pay customs charges on my order?







No. All EU orders are shipped duty paid from our distribution centre in Germany. This means there are no additional customs charges, import duties or taxes to pay upon delivery.

- Can I buy replacement bottles for Special Fills and Limited Editions?







Special Fills are one off runs, and Limited Editions are capped at specific production numbers. As a result, replacement bottles are not always available. These products are covered by our standard two year warranty.

- Are bulbs included with my lava lamp?







Most Mathmos lava lamps are supplied with one bulb as standard. Occasionally, a complimentary bulb may be included due to the fragile nature of lava lamp bulbs and the risk of damage during transit. For specific details, please refer to the product page specifications for your lamp.

- Can I refill my lava lamp bottle myself?







No. Mathmos lava lamp bottles are sealed units and should not be opened or refilled. Attempting to refill a lava lamp bottle may damage the lamp and void your warranty.

- Can I cancel my order?







Once an order has been placed, it is processed quickly and changes or cancellations are not always possible. Please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/) as soon as possible if you need to change or cancel your order, and we will do our best to help.

- Can I buy a lava lamp as a gift?







Yes. Mathmos lava lamps make great gifts and can be shipped directly to an alternative delivery address. Pricing is not shown on the packing slip, and all lamps are supplied in a stylish gift box. Please note that we do not currently offer gift wrapping.

- Will sold out Limited Editions be restocked?







Limited Editions are produced in fixed quantities and will not be restocked. To avoid missing out on future releases, we recommend signing up to our [email newsletter](https://mathmos.us3.list-manage.com/subscribe/post?u=cb9394b9c4b6ddd0a6a43cd62&id=4180d60836&v_id=452&f_id=0087c2e1f0) and following us on [social media](https://www.instagram.com/mathmos_originals/).

- Will Special Fills be available again?







[Special Fills](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamp-colours-mathmos-special-fills-explained/) are one off runs, but similar colourways may occasionally return. Follow us on [social media](https://www.instagram.com/mathmos_originals/) and subscribe to our [email newsletter](https://mathmos.us3.list-manage.com/subscribe/post?u=cb9394b9c4b6ddd0a6a43cd62&id=4180d60836&v_id=452&f_id=0087c2e1f0) for updates.

- How long does delivery take?







Standard delivery takes 2 to 5 working days. We also offer next working day delivery at checkout for an additional charge. For full details, please see our [Delivery & Returns](https://mathmos.com/delivery-and-returns/) page.

- How long does delivery take to the EU?







EU website orders are typically delivered within 2 to 8 working days, depending on the destination country. You can view a full list of EU delivery times by country on our [delivery information](https://mathmos.eu/delivery-and-returns/) page.

- What is your returns policy?

Extended Christmas Returns: Products shipped between 1 November and 24 December may be returned for a refund or exchange, provided we are notified by 30 January. All other return terms remain unchanged.

- Why is my lava lamp not working?







If your lava lamp is not working, the most common cause is a failed bulb. First, check the fuse in your plug and ensure the bulb is correctly fitted. If your lamp was supplied with a spare bulb, we recommend trying this as well.



Mathmos bulbs are covered by a one year guarantee. If all bulbs supplied with your lamp have failed within this period, please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/contact-us/) for support. Should your bulbs have reached the end of their lifespan, [replacement bulbs](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/lava-lamp-bulbs/) are available to purchase from our website.

- Are lava lamps dangerous?







No, Mathmos lava lamps are safe and designed to meet applicable safety standards. All Mathmos lava lamps undergo regular testing by independent bodies.



Lava lamps are designed to generate heat, which is essential for creating the flowing lava effect. During use, the bottle and metal parts can become hot, so always place your lamp on a stable surface and follow the instructions provided.



For best performance and longevity, use the correct bulb and do not exceed the recommended operating times.


- How do I fit my halogen bulb?







Halogen bulbs are very delicate. Please find instructions relevant to your lamp [here](https://mathmos.com/instructions/).

- How long should it take for my lava lamp to warm up?







Most lava lamps should start working within one hour to an hour and a half from being switched on. First, they will form stalagmite shapes before the liquid lava formations. Please note that new bottles may take longer to heat up when new (up to three hours), but the time taken is expected to decrease after several uses.

Yes. We had these specially made to ensure our 60s lamps can still be enjoyed, see [here](https://mathmos.com/product/astro-lava-lamp-bulbs-35w-sbc-bayonet/).

Mathmos bottles are supplied with a lip that runs around the bottom of the bottle to ensure they sit straight in the base. When the bottle is placed on a base, it should fit securely, although it will still be possible to move the bottle from side to side, and a shadow gap may be visible. There have been many bottle and base size variations over time, so it may be possible with some much older products that a new bottle does not fit in the base. Please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/Contact-us/) for advice if your bottle isn't sitting on the base.

Some lamps have a shadow gap between the base and the bottle; this is normal.

Holding the bottle and arm piece carefully, lift and remove the bottle and arm piece from the base. Carefully take the arm piece off the bottle. When installing the bulb, please only fit the correct type, as stated in the label on the base. You can install this by gently pushing them into the holder socket. - Are Mathmos lava lamps suitable for children?







The only suitable lava lamps for children are the [Mathmos Neo Table](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/lava-lamps-for-children-and-all-ages/) range. The Neo lava lamp was developed with children in mind, so as well as looking stylish, it is also shatterproof, has fixture points, does not get too hot, and is screwed together as a secure unit. The lamp has also passed stringent safety checks required to be classified as child-safe, something no other lava lamp in the UK has a classification for. We recommend all other lava lamps be placed well out of reach of children under 14 years old.

- What makes Neo qualify for a certificate for all ages, unlike other lava lamps?







The product must adhere to a stringent criterion to achieve this. For example, the bulb should not be accessible without a tool, it must be low voltage (not mains voltage), it must be very stable, it must have a shatterproof coating on the glass, the bottle must be fixed to the base, and it must not be too hot.

- Can I use a Neo bottle in my Telstar or Astro Baby lava lamps?







No. Neo has a unique formulation different from other bottles. This would overheat if used with other models. Contrastingly, Astro Baby and Telstar bottles will run slowly or not at all if used with the Neo.

- Can I use other candles in the product?







Astro Flame is designed for use with a tealight only.

- How do I clean the heat diffuser?







Clean the heat diffuser with a lint-free cloth between uses and avoid using chemicals or abrasives. It is normal for the diffuser to discolour over time.


- What size image is produced by the Space Projector?







The scale of the focused image achieved will vary depending on the effect. Lava lamp wheels project focused images of up to 4 meters / 13 feet in a dark room. All other wheels will present a crisp, focused image up to 1.5 meters / 4 feet. Please note that a darker environment is required to achieve a larger image.

- Can the Space Projector be displayed on the ceiling or floor?







The Space Projector can be cast onto a ceiling by rotating the body but cannot be angled toward the floor.

- Can I use a lava lamp wheel and graphic wheel simultaneously?







No, only one wheel at a time. However, you can use wheels in conjunction with graphic or coloured slides to achieve more possibilities.

- Can the Space Projector be mounted on a wall?







No. The Space Projector was designed to be positioned on a flat surface.

- Can I buy a Space Projector without an effect?







No. The [Space Projector](https://mathmos.com/product/lava-lamp-projector-silver/) includes one effect of your choice as standard. You can also purchase alternative effects separately [here](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/projector-effects/).

- Can I buy replacement parts for the Space Projector?







Yes. We offer replacement bulbs, focus bodies, and slide carriers [here](https://mathmos.com/lava-lamps/spares-extras/space-projector-parts/). For any other part queries, please contact our [customer service team](https://mathmos.com/Contact-us/).

- How do I make the image from my Space Projector clearer?







Turn the clear plastic focus body on the front of your Space Projector to adjust the image and focus. Please refer to our [video here](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AM0df-mvhcU&t=12s) if unsure.

- There is something strange in my lava lamp wheel, what is it?







Run the Space Projector wheel for several days, at least three hours per use, and this should disappear. If this has not helped please read our [Faulty Product Returns Advice](https://mathmos.com/Faulty-products/) and contact our [Customer Services Team](https://mathmos.com/Contact-us/) for help with a product within warranty.

- My Space Projector is making a noise, what shall I do?







Ensure that your Space Projector is positioned on an even surface and is not run for more than 6 hours. If the noise continues and your lamp was purchased from us less than two years ago, please read our [Faulty Product Returns Advice](https://mathmos.com/Faulty-products/).

- I think my Space Projector is faulty, what should I do?







Firstly, please read through our FAQs to see if your question has been answered. If not, we do offer a two-year warranty on all lamps purchased directly from the Mathmos website. If your product is still covered, you can contact our [Customer Service Team](https://mathmos.com/Contact-us/) for assistance. Please also read our [Faulty Product Returns Advice](https://mathmos.com/Faulty-products/).

- Can I use 35 mm film in my Space Projector?







Yes. You can use these in the slide carrier, but please note that this can fade the image over time.

# Mathmos Not Available To The USA?

Everyone kept talking about mathmos so I decided to go look. They are only available in the UK and not the USA. How come?

Couple of questions - I never heard of them until I found this site. I always thought Lava International was the creator. Is mathmos a distributor or a new company all together? Confused.. :\

Lastly, is the mathmos clear/white large globe the same size as the 52oz here? Where would one pick it up here in the states?

Thanks

Kris

Hi Kris, Mathmos do not ship to the USA. It is probably down to the high shipping costs to send a lamp all the way to the USA. They are by far the best Lava Lamp producers in my opinion. The only way you can get hold of a Mathmos Lamp in the USA is to find an E-bay seller in the UK that is prepared to ship to the USA like myself. I have a number of lamps for sale at the moment and I will be happy to ship to the USA.

To add to what others have said, Mathmos (earlier known as Crestworth, Ltd.), founded by Edward Craven Walker, was the creator of what we now know as the lava lamp.  The units available in the USA are obviously, quite different.

This situation traces to an agreement made in the early 1960s.  My understanding is that the Americans who bought rights to sell a 'lava lamp' exclusively in the USA first saw the Mathmos lamps in Germany.  Here is the story as summarized by an online webpage from the Lemelson-MIT program (Lemelson-MIT is dedicated to aiding entrepreneurs):

"Walker began marketing the lamp in Europe in 1963 with the name Astro Lamp. Its debut was perfectly timed to precede the “psychedelic craze” of the 1960s. He patented the design in England in 1964, and the lamps began selling first in London at Selfridges and Fortnum and Mason. Walker set up a manufacturing plant in Poole, England under the name of Crestworth, Ltd.

The following year, at a trade show in Hamburg, Germany, American executives bought rights to manufacture the lamps in North America. They began selling “Lava Lite” lamps via their Chicago-based Lava Brand Motion Lamp Company, which later became Lava Manufacturing Corp. Within just a few years, lava lamp had exceeded seven million units worldwide."

Sadly, those rights were, I believe, 'in perpetuity' (someone else might have more information on this than do I).

For some reason, I believe Mathmos decided that they could sell a version of their Telstar model in the USA at some point in the late 1990s or early 2000s.  I  assume that is because the Telstar is the model least like those sold currently in the USA, which more closely resemble the Mathmos Astro model.  Mathmos did have an agreement to sell at least 100 USA models (wired for 120V) and they did so through a USA distributor.

Those units, priced reasonably at something under $100, came with a 'certificate' indicating that they were some of the first Mathmos lamps to be available for sale in the USA.  As it happens, I own a small set of these lamps.  They are identical to the UK models except, of course, for the wiring and the bulbs.  However not long after these became available, they were - suddenly - no longer for sale from the single distributor.  My assumption is that lawyers representing the USA Lava Lite people quickly put an end to that marketing maneuver.  I have no idea whether fines or penalties were involved, or how this was resolved, but clearly Mathmos has not attempted to sell in the USA subsequently.

It's sad really, because I am sure there would be good demand here in the USA if Mathmos could sell directly to USA consumers.

If you want a Mathmos original lamp, my advance is to use a 3rd party to get it shipped to the States. Then just buy a small voltage converter; it is easier I believe, than rewiring it yourself and if you get tired of the lamp, it is probably worth more without a conversion.

Hi,

Rewiring would probably not be necessary, get a plug adaptor (travel adaptor) for a couple of $$ or swap the plug to a US one and then change the bulb, the wiring should easily handle the current of even a 40W 110V bulb - unless you're going for a Neo which are 12V in which case you just need a 12V DC Power supply with correct plug and polarity and enough amps (about 2A as I recall), could probably be run off a car battery.

The Smart Astro would be the same (if you can ever find one) except opposite polarity so never use a Neo power supply on them as they didn't fit any sort of polarity protection on them (All it takes is 4 diodes arranged as a 'bridge' costing less than £/$1 \[retail price, trade/manufacturing cost would be pennies\] and taking very little space - sadly almost no manufacturers ever bother with them, not even on Laptops costing $$$$.$$)

Tim

(Hereford, UK)

web.title: Lava lamp
web.description: A lava lamp is a decorative lamp that was invented in 1963 by British entrepreneur Edward Craven Walker, the founder of the lighting company Mathmos.
web.markdown:
# Lava lamp Lava lamp Decorative lamp [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Lava_lamps_%2816136876840%29.jpg/960px-Lava_lamps_%2816136876840%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Lava_lamps_(16136876840).jpg) Lava lamps[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/1990s_Mathmos_Astro.jpg/500px-1990s_Mathmos_Astro.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:1990s_Mathmos_Astro.jpg) An original [Mathmos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathmos "Mathmos") _Astro_ lava lamp A **lava lamp** is a decorative [lamp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light_fixture "Light fixture") that was invented in 1963 by British entrepreneur [Edward Craven Walker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edward_Craven_Walker "Edward Craven Walker"), the founder of the lighting company [Mathmos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathmos "Mathmos"). Lava lamps consist of a [bolus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bolus "wikt:bolus") of wax and a clear or translucent liquid inside a glass vessel. The vessel is placed on a lamp base with an [incandescent light bulb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incandescent_light_bulb "Incandescent light bulb"). The heat causes temporary reductions in the wax's density and the liquid's surface tension. As the warmed wax rises through the liquid, it cools, loses its buoyancy, and falls back to the bottom of the vessel in a cycle that is visually suggestive of [pāhoehoe lava](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava#P%C4%81hoehoe "Lava"). The lamps are designed in a variety of styles and colours. Lava lamps are commonly associated with [hippie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hippie "Hippie"), [psychedelia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychedelia "Psychedelia"), and [cannabis cultures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cannabis_culture "Cannabis culture").[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-1)[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-2) ## Operation [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Blue_Lava_lamp.JPG/500px-Blue_Lava_lamp.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Blue_Lava_lamp.JPG) A cyan lava lamp A classic lava lamp contains a standard incandescent or [halogen lamp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Halogen_lamp "Halogen lamp") which heats a tall (often tapered) glass bottle. A formula from a 1968 US patent consisted of water and a transparent, [translucent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transparency_(optics) "Transparency (optics)"), or opaque mix of mineral oil, [paraffin wax](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paraffin_wax "Paraffin wax"), and [carbon tetrachloride](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_tetrachloride "Carbon tetrachloride").[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-pat156-3) The clear water or mineral oil can optionally be coloured with translucent [dyes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dye "Dye"). Common wax has a [density](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Density "Density") much lower than water and floats at any temperature. Carbon tetrachloride is denser than water (also [nonflammable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonflammable "Nonflammable") and [miscible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miscible "Miscible") with wax) and can be added to increase the wax density at room temperature. When heated, the wax expands, becomes less dense, and rises; as it cools, its density increases and it sinks.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-pat156-3) The wax also becomes fluid, causing blobs to ascend to the top of the lamp.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-pat156-3) A metallic wire coil in the bottle's base breaks the cooled blobs' [surface tension](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_tension "Surface tension"), allowing them to merge and repeat the cycle. Video of an orange lava lamp in operation Since 1970, lava lamps made for the US market have not used carbon tetrachloride, the use of which was banned in the country that year due to toxicity.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-4) Haggerty, their current manufacturer, has stated that their current formulation is a [trade secret](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade_secret "Trade secret").[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-5) The underlying [fluid mechanics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fluid_mechanics "Fluid mechanics") phenomenon in lava lamps is a form of [Rayleigh–Taylor instability](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93Taylor_instability "Rayleigh–Taylor instability"). Agitating the lamp once the wax has melted causes the two fluids to [emulsify](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emulsion "Emulsion"), resulting in a cloudy appearance. Some emulsification occurs during the lamp cycle; however, the wax reforms into a single mass upon cooling. Severe cases can require many heating-cooling cycles to clear.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-6) In 2015, a new design was introduced that uses [ferrofluid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrofluid "Ferrofluid") in place of wax.[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-7) ## History [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Lava_Lamp_Factory_Chicago_IL.jpg/960px-Lava_Lamp_Factory_Chicago_IL.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Lava_Lamp_Factory_Chicago_IL.jpg) Former Lava Lamp factory, at 1650 [Irving Park Road](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois_Route_19 "Illinois Route 19"), [Chicago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") British entrepreneur [Edward Craven Walker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edward_Craven_Walker "Edward Craven Walker") conceived the lava lamp after observing a homemade egg timer made from a cocktail shaker bubbling on a pub stove.[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-8) This precursor was designed and patented [GBpatent 703924](https://worldwide.espacenet.com/textdoc?DB=EPODOC&IDX=GB703924) by Donald Dunnet.[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-9) Crestworth later launched the first 'Astro' lava lamp in 1963.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-10) After the lamp's commercial launch, Craven Walker worked with British inventor David George Smith, who is listed as the inventor on [U.S. Patent 3,387,396](https://patents.google.com/patent/US3387396) for a 'Display Device,' filed in 1965, issued in 1968, and assigned to Craven Walker's company, Crestworth Ltd, based in [Poole, Dorset](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poole "Poole"). In 1965, Adolph Wertheimer and Hy Spector were intrigued by Walker's product when they saw it at a German trade show. They bought the American rights and began the Lava Manufacturing Corporation in [Chicago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") to sell what they called the Lava Lite Lamp. In the late 1970s, US rights were sold to Larry Haggerty, who created a subsidiary of his company, Haggerty Enterprises, called Lava World International, which produced American lava lamps for over 30 years.[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-11) In 2003, American lava lamp maker Lava World International (formerly Lava-Simplex-Scribe Internationale) moved its production to China. In 2008, it was acquired by Talon Merchant Capital and its name was changed to Lava Lite LLC. As of 2016, lava lamps were being sold under Lifespan brands in North America.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-12) In 2018, the "Lava Lamp" brand was acquired by toy and gift maker Schylling Inc. of North Andover, MA.[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-13) who continue to hold the US trademark rights to both the shape and name of LAVA lamp.[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-14) When the American rights were sold, Craven Walker retained the rights for the rest of the world and took on two business partners in the late 1980s, [Cressida Granger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cressida_Granger_(entrepreneur) "Cressida Granger (entrepreneur)") and David Mulley. They eventually took over the company and changed its name to [Mathmos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathmos "Mathmos") in 1992. Edward Craven Walker remained a consultant to them until his death in 2000. Mathmos continues to make Lava Lamps and related products. They have won two Queens Awards for Export, and the Best Multi-Media prize at the Design Week awards.[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-15)[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-Bachelor-16) Astro lava lamp was launched in 1963 and celebrated its 50th anniversary in 2013.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-17) Mathmos lava lamps have been made in [Poole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poole "Poole"), [Dorset](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dorset "Dorset"), since 1963. ## See also - [Bubble light](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bubble_light "Bubble light")– Bubbling decorative light - [Lavarand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lavarand "Lavarand")– Random number generator - [Liquid light show](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquid_light_show "Liquid light show")– Psychedelic art form - [Plasma globe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasma_globe "Plasma globe")– Decorative electrical device ## References 01. metadata.og:title: Lava lamp
metadata.ogDescription: A lava lamp is a decorative lamp that was invented in 1963 by British entrepreneur Edward Craven Walker, the founder of the lighting company Mathmos. Lava lamps consist of a bolus of wax and a clear or translucent liquid inside a glass vessel. The vessel is placed on a lamp base with an incandescent light bulb. The heat causes temporary reductions in the wax's density and the liquid's surface tension. As the warmed wax rises through the liquid, it cools, loses its buoyancy, and falls back to the bottom of the vessel in a cycle that is visually suggestive of pāhoehoe lava. The lamps are designed in a variety of styles and colours. Lava lamps are commonly associated with hippie, psychedelia, and cannabis cultures.
